summary: Jeff Atwood celebrates Microsoft's announcement that Windows 7 will include a virtualized copy of Windows XP, seeing it as a significant step toward breaking free from backwards compatibility constraints. He argues that virtualization technology has matured enough to make running legacy applications seamless, with the virtual OS becoming invisible to users. While acknowledging Windows 7's implementation isn't as polished as competitors like Parallels, he views this as an important milestone that could free the industry from the burden of maintaining ancient APIs and allow genuine innovation in operating system design.
key_points: Windows 7 shipping with a licensed virtual XP copy represents a major shift in how Microsoft handles backwards compatibility
Windows XP's minimal system requirements (233 MHz, 64 MB RAM) make it trivially easy to virtualize on modern hardware
Modern virtualization solutions like Parallels' coherence mode can make virtualized applications appear native to users
Virtualization offers 'nearly perfect backwards compatibility' by running the actual legacy OS rather than emulating old APIs
The demand for perfect backwards compatibility has historically held back software innovation
money_quotes: The demand for perfect backwards compatibility has held the industry back for too long.
It frees software developers from the crushing weight of their own historical software mistakes.
The attraction of virtualizing older operating systems is that it throws off the eternal yoke of backwards compatibility.
While XP may have been the apple of 2001's eye, in computing dog years, it's basically... dead.
From the user's perspective, it's just another application in a window on their desktop. They don't need to know or care if the application is running in a virtual machine.

Instead of bending over backwards to make sure you never break any old APIs, you can build new systems free of the contortions and compromises inherent in guaranteeing that new versions of the operating system never break old applications.
key_insight: Virtualization technology enables operating systems to finally break free from the crippling burden of backwards compatibility by running legacy systems alongside new ones, rather than forever supporting ancient APIs.
